channel.title: Psychology Today – Latest
channel.description: Latest articles from Psychology Today (full content)

item.title: 2 Ways to Increase Your Capacity for Joy
item.description:
Happiness

2 Ways to Increase Your Capacity for Joy

You may need to retrain your nervous system to tolerate joy.

Posted March 27, 2026

Source: Sasha Freemind / Unsplash

Joy is culturally portrayed as a high-vibe state that you are supposed to reach and then, somehow, maintain indefinitely. Just one scroll through any social platform is enough to reveal how modern society has framed joy as something you “perform.”

The issue with this understanding of joy is that it sends the wrong message: if you are not feeling good, you must not be trying hard enough. Joy is supposed to be an automatic nervous system response for when you feel safe, resourced, and connected. And like any other biological capacity, it can be strengthened, but not through pressure, denial, or relentless positivity.

Think of joy the way you would a muscle tissue. You do not grow muscle by simply wanting it to be bigger or stronger. You grow it by providing the right conditions, like enough load to stimulate growth, enough rest to recover, and enough nourishment to sustain the system. Joy works much the same way. It’s not an attitude you adopt alone but a response your mind and body generate when the conditions are right.

Here are two evidence-based ways to strengthen your capacity for joy, without gaslighting yourself into “being grateful” or pretending everything is fine.

1. Train Your Nervous System to Tolerate Joy

It may seem strange, but one of the biggest barriers to joy is intolerance for positive emotion, not sadness, as we most often think.

Just as people have varying capacities for distress, they also have differing levels of tolerance for how much pleasure, ease, and excitement their nervous systems can safely hold. For many people, especially those who grew up with emotional neglect, unpredictability, or chronic criticism, feeling good does not feel safe. Instead, it might make you feel vulnerable or exposed, or, in many cases, it might feel painfully temporary.

In a large study of young adults, people with higher levels of adverse childhood experiences reported significantly lower levels of happiness in adulthood. But, more important, it wasn’t just because they felt more distress. It was because their emotional systems had been shaped in ways that made adaptive processing of emotion harder.

This means they were less likely to use strategies like cognitive reappraisal (the ability to reinterpret situations in a way that allows positive meaning and emotional relief) and more likely to rely on emotional suppression, a strategy that reliably predicts lower well-being. Early adversity, in a way, shrinks the nervous system’s capacity to access and sustain positive emotion.

So, when joy begins to arise, their systems interrupt it, birthing thoughts like:

  • “This won’t last.”
  • “Something bad is about to happen.”
  • “Don’t get too excited.”
  • “Enjoy it now before it goes away.”

Such pessimistic responses are actually protective reflexes built by a nervous system that is working overtime. A nervous system that learned, long ago, that good moments were followed by disappointment, withdrawal, or instability. As a result, it developed the tendency to stay guarded, even in pleasure.

The guardedness is a natural consequence of the threat system (centered around the amygdala) being tuned by early stress. Since this system is designed to scan for danger even during positive moments, it pulls attention back toward vigilance. The result is a shortened, brittle experience of joy that never quite gets to settle in the body.

The goal, then, is not to create more positive experiences but to increase your nervous system’s capacity to stay with them. One of the simplest ways to do this is through positive affect savoring, which is the deliberate practice of lingering in a pleasant sensation for just a little longer than you normally would. For example:

  • When you take a sip of coffee you enjoy, pause.
  • When someone says something kind, let it land.
  • When you feel a moment of ease, notice it in your chest or shoulders.

Even 10 to 15 seconds of sustained attention to a positive sensation begins to strengthen the neural pathways associated with safety and reward. It’s a way of training your nervous system to learn that just because an experience is new, it does not mean it is dangerous, and you don’t have to shut down. Practice this regularly and see joy imperceptibly lasting a little longer than usual.

2. Reduce the Cognitive Noise that Smothers Joy

Joy can also feel elusive when it’s drowned out by other mental clutter. You might be having a good moment, but part of your mind might already be rehearsing what comes next, or thinking about what you forgot to do, or what you should be worrying about. What joy really requires is conscious presence.

This is not just a philosophical idea; it shows up clearly in experimental research. In a randomized controlled trial with university students, just two weeks of daily mindfulness training led to significant reductions in anxiety, stress, negative emotion, and, crucially, long-term reductions in rumination that became even stronger three months later.

The intervention didn’t magically remove life stressors. What it changed was the mind’s habit of spinning, rehearsing, and looping around them. In other words, when the rumination quietened down, well-being automatically rose.

This helps explain why, when the mind is loud, joy often tends to disappear. Or when attention is constantly fragmented or pulled into past regrets, future worries, or self-monitoring, positive moments pass through too quickly to register. Mindfulness, on the other hand, works by reducing the cognitive interference that blocks it.

One of the most effective ways to begin doing this is not meditation in the traditional sense but something far more practical: monotasking.

Pick one small activity per day to do without multitasking, such as eating without scrolling, or walking without listening to anything, or showering without planning your day. You will notice that when the mind stops narrating for a moment, the nervous system can finally register what has been true all along: that something good is happening right now.

A version of this post also appears on Forbes.com.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Mark Travers Ph.D.

item.title: What the Meta and YouTube Lawsuits Say About Kids and Social
item.description:
Social Media

What the Meta and YouTube Lawsuits Say About Kids and Social

Here are some things we can do to help children that big tech will not.

Posted March 27, 2026

Key points
  • U.S. juries have said that Meta and YouTube harm children's mental health and safety.
  • Big tech companies know that their platforms pose risks to children but don't provide sufficient safegaurds.
  • Addictive design principles can leave children at risk of harm and mental distress.
  • There are things that each of us can do to help children stay healthy in a world driven by social media.

This week, jury findings in California and New Mexico place a spotlight on social media that all users could benefit from paying attention to. Jurors in New Mexico concluded that Meta (which owns Instagram, Facebook, and WhatsApp) had engaged in what NPR states as “‘unconscionable’ trade practices that unfairly took advantage of the vulnerabilities of and inexperience of children,” finding thousands of violations of the state’s Unfair Practices Act.

In California, a 20-year-old female plaintiff won her lawsuit against Meta and YouTube (owned by Google), claiming that the addictive design of both platforms, which she engaged with excessively as a young child, caused mental distress and harm. Speaking volumes about culpability, TikTok and Snap (owner of Snapchat) settled with her before the case went to trial.

While research has been clear about the complex correlational and causal relationship between social media use and mental health for a while now, the outcome of these lawsuits points to important realities that all of us, too often, ignore.

In my 20 years of researching, writing, and speaking about technology use and mental health, I’ve never had someone comment, “I feel totally in control of the time I spend and the material presented to me in social media spaces, and I feel great about both.” More often than not, I hear the opposite.

People of all ages are increasingly aware of feelings of agitation, anxiety, depression, and overwhelm in relation to their social media engagement, while, at the same time, “losing” more time there than ever before. Even still, they feel largely incapable of stepping away from it.

Most of the devices we own, as well as the digital spaces they offer us, are designed with a hidden curriculum that functions to keep us on screen and in app for as long as possible. When we rely on our smartphones for a majority of our daily human needs (time-keeping, entertainment, communication or connection, education, shopping, and health-related data), we play into the hands of this curriculum. When app developers offer us the opportunity to do web searching, messaging, purchasing, posting, and more within their app, they are designing to the curriculum, making our in-app experience so smooth that we lose track of time spent and actions taken therein.

Without consciously realizing that we are becoming increasingly loyal to and dependent upon our devices and apps, we unconsciously hand over significant data to powerful forces connected to capitalism, comparison culture, and algorithms designed by everyone but human development experts. This, in turn, informs our algorithm about what kind of content we’ll pay attention to and take action on. Too often, in large and small ways, this includes information about our insecurities and anxieties as well as the places in which we feel less-than in comparison to a world of curated images, ideas, and accomplishments. This is a recipe for compromised mental health.

Having been presented with admitted evidence, the New Mexico and California juries are telling us that big tech companies know that their platforms:

1. Put children at risk

2. Are designed with addictive elements that are harmful to the developing brains of children and youth

3. Are offered without warning or protections anyway

Every one of us, regardless of age or any other demographic, is likely connected to children and youth for whom social media is important. For many of them, social media use will, at some point or another, be a requirement for their academic, vocational, or relational well-being. If we hope to help them mitigate the potential negative effects on their mental health, there are a few actions we can take.

Have non-shaming conversations with young people about the place social media has in their lives

For many youth, social media spaces are a primary place in which they maintain their relationships. Ask them about this and help them find ways of balancing their digital relationships with some that exist in time and space proximity to them. Help them identify at least one person that they have physical access to that they could go to in a time of crisis. Let them know that it’s OK if it isn’t you, but that it would be wise for this to be a person who has what is needed to guide them in these moments.

Explore social media together, identifying the things that make it helpful as well as those that make it harmful (keeping a non-shaming frame in mind)

Find times to scroll through social media together, making a game of identifying accounts or people who use it well and those who do not. Award points for finding filtered images, AI-generated images that aren’t marked as such, and bullying or violent language. Make it fun and give prizes. The goal is to help young people develop discernment skills to engage while in social media spaces.

Offer non-digital experiences to young people that are as fiery (if not more so) as digital ones

Social media is here to stay, which means that there will always be a place for young people to go to trigger the release of dopamine. Consider the kinds of things your young person enjoys and seek out the most wild and edgy form of that in places that aren’t digital.

Does your young person love racing games? Take them to a racetrack to drive a car or watch a race. Sponsor a ballroom or hip hop class. Buy a gamer a ticket to a particularly edgy play. Keep looking for immersive and relevant embodied experiences.

Model embodied connections with others (being with others in the same space without phones)

Make awkwardness a normal part of interpersonal relationships. Invite and empower young people to do the same.

Avoiding social anxiety and awkwardness is a huge part of what keeps us scrolling rather than seeking out in-person opportunities. Let the young people in your life see you trying to be more embodied and let them witness you living through awkward moments. With their guidance, help them create spaces in which they can do the same.

Educate Yourself About AI

Ask young people their thoughts and feelings about it. Use AI only with deep critical thought and understanding about its potential risks (especially for youth and children).

As social media is increasingly run with AI technologies, it is more important than ever for all of us to understand how unintelligent AI actually is at providing advice or “companionship” to young people. This topic requires more than can be offered in summary here. For now, check out this resource or this resource to begin your education.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Doreen Dodgen-Magee, Psy.D.
